jobs.title: After-School Program Director, Alpha - $200,000/year USD
jobs.description:
On-site at an Alpha campus in New York City, NY; Miami, FL; or San Francisco, CA — we provide relocation support
$200,000 per year
Full-time position

Traditional elementary schools reduce expectations when children face difficulties. Your approach is the opposite. You know that six-year-olds can accomplish far more than conventional classrooms demand, and you have used performance data to demonstrate this repeatedly.

Alpha's model allocates two hours to learning. Adaptive software handles academic instruction during morning sessions, freeing your schedule for the interventions that genuinely transform children: motivation, life skills development, coaching, and accountability structures. You take direct ownership of a K-3 student cohort while also coaching the Guides—Alpha's term for the adults who mentor and inspire students—who drive their day-to-day progress. Your role keeps you actively engaged daily, not directing operations from a separate office.

Your typical day alternates among three core activities. You analyze performance metrics and Coachbot data to identify students and Guides falling behind targets. You conduct coaching sessions that conclude with concrete next actions rather than general encouragement. And you lead workshops for groups of five- to nine-year-olds covering topics like public speaking, sustained attention, or peer feedback skills.

When you identify effective methods, you document them. The frameworks you create establish the standard that all other Guides implement, extending your influence from a single cohort to an entire grade level. Demonstrating your ability to replicate that quality at scale is the pathway to broader leadership responsibilities here.

If reducing expectations seems compassionate to you, this role is not appropriate. If increasing them feels like showing respect, submit your application.

What You Will Be Doing

Using performance data, classroom observation, and direct feedback to coach both Guides and students, then maintaining accountability to quantifiable targets for each group
Leading one-hour live workshops for K-3 students focused on life skills including public speaking, sustained focus, teamwork, resilience, accountability, and peer feedback
Conducting individual and small-group sessions that link each student's personal goals and interests to tangible academic advancement
Creating and iterating on playbooks that convert your most effective practices into repeatable frameworks other Guides can implement reliably
Training Guides on these playbooks and demonstrating facilitation excellence so quality standards persist as the team expands

What You Won’t Be Doing

Teaching academic content or presenting lectures; adaptive technology handles instruction while you focus on coaching people
Reducing expectations when students encounter challenges
Managing from a distance the way instructional coaches and school administrators typically do; you remain embedded with your cohort every day
Managing the full campus budget, operations infrastructure, and parent communication; a Campus Lead handles those areas so you concentrate on student and Guide performance
Advocating for traditional pedagogical approaches or adhering to standard K-12 curricula

After-School Program Director Key Responsibilities

Fulfill Alpha's three core commitments to every K-3 student: they develop a love of school, achieve learning at twice the standard rate, and cultivate outstanding life skills and self-sufficiency.

Basic Requirements

Available to work on-site at an Alpha campus in New York City, NY; Miami, FL; or San Francisco, CA; we provide relocation support
Bachelor's degree in Education, Psychology, Business, Organizational Leadership, Child Development, or a related discipline
Minimum of 5 years' experience working with elementary-aged children (approximately ages 5 to 9)
Demonstrated team leadership experience with 5 or more direct reports, including hiring and performance-based removal authority
Proven experience building, launching, or substantially scaling a program, team, school, or initiative from inception, rather than managing an established operation
Willingness to work in an educational environment that rejects conventional teaching methodologies
Legal authorization to work in the United States

Nice-to-have Requirements

Master's degree in a relevant discipline
Demonstrated ability using metrics, dashboards, or data analytics to identify issues and produce measurable improvements
History of coaching or managing adults toward quantifiable results, beyond personal execution
Experience in youth development programs, camp leadership, competitive athletics coaching, tutoring, or enrichment settings where motivating participants was essential
Proven ability facilitating sessions or speaking publicly to groups of young children

About Alpha

Join the team building school around student growth

Alpha reimagines the school day around faster learning, stronger mentorship, and more time for students to build real-world skills.

Roles with real student impact:
Alpha hires teachers (guides), operators, admissions experts, school & curriculum leaders, and remote team members.

Education work that pays like it matters:
Alpha offers six-figure roles for people ready to help build a radically better school experience.

A culture built for builders:
Alpha is fast-moving, ambitious, and student focused. It’s for people who want to improve the system, not maintain it.

Alpha is where you can do the most meaningful work of your career.

jobs.title: Entrepreneurship Program Director, Founders School - $250,000/year USD
jobs.description:
Your job is to develop founders, not deliver curriculum.

Most schools prepare students for a future that remains hypothetical. We believe the future begins immediately. Founders School operates as a four-year high school designed for students unwilling to postpone their ambitions until adulthood. These students do not engage in simulated business exercises. They establish genuine companies, collaborate with experienced founders, develop mastery in AI, and address real-world challenges. Our expectations are elevated because our students possess elevated capabilities: launch a business generating one million dollars in revenue, uncover problems deserving attention, and achieve AI proficiency ahead of most adults. Your responsibility is to ensure that ambition translates into sustained execution.

You will not deliver traditional instruction or dictate operational strategy to students. Instead, you will coach a cohort of young entrepreneurs daily, ensuring they maintain accountability, navigate obstacles, and sustain momentum when entrepreneurship becomes challenging. You will recognize the right moments to push them harder, offer encouragement, or facilitate introductions to mentors and resources. The curriculum, Entrepreneurs-in-Residence, and founder network supply the technical and strategic guidance. You supply the structure, expectations, and drive that convert capability into measurable outcomes.

This role also represents a chance to construct something foundational. As part of our founding team in New York City, you will influence the culture, operational systems, and student experience at a school reimagining what secondary education can achieve. Your contributions will extend beyond a single cohort. They will inform the framework for every subsequent Founders School campus. If you are motivated by the startup ecosystem, believe rigorous standards unlock exceptional performance, and wish to dedicate your efforts to cultivating the next generation of entrepreneurs, we invite your application.

What You Will Be Doing

Provide coaching and accountability to a cohort of student entrepreneurs through weekly one-on-one sessions, daily progress reviews, and group check-ins that ensure consistent, measurable advancement.
Maintain exceptionally high standards for execution while delivering the guidance and accountability required to help students navigate challenges and continue building.
Track student progress within FounderOS, recognize when momentum stalls, and facilitate connections to mentors, Entrepreneurs-in-Residence, or additional resources that drive accelerated development.
Contribute to the creation of culture, recurring traditions, workshops, events, and the operational cadence of a world-class entrepreneurial high school.
Accompany students on travel experiences several times annually while cultivating enduring relationships with founders, investors, operators, and entrepreneurial families.

What You Won’t Be Doing

Serving as the primary business strategist or consultant for student ventures. That expertise comes from FounderOS, Entrepreneurs-in-Residence, and external mentors.
Delivering conventional academic instruction or assessing traditional coursework.
Tolerating mediocre effort from students with the capacity for outstanding achievement.
Leading workshops on topics outside your domain of expertise or defaulting to generic entrepreneurial guidance.
Operating within an inflexible institutional framework where administrative processes obstruct meaningful student progress.

Entrepreneurship Program Director Key Responsibilities

Establish an environment in which every student advances consistently as an entrepreneur by integrating outstanding coaching, rigorous accountability, strategic mentorship connections, and a culture that transforms ambitious teenagers into competent founders.

Basic Requirements

A minimum of 3 years in a leadership or high-impact role within a startup or high-growth business.
Sound business judgment and the confidence to engage meaningfully with early-stage founders while recognizing when to escalate to subject matter experts.
Proven capacity to coach, inspire, and maintain high accountability with top-performing individuals.
Regular, practical experience leveraging AI to enhance both the quality and efficiency of your work.
Exceptional communication and relationship-building capabilities with students, families, mentors, and founders.
Availability to work full-time on-site in New York City, including occasional travel throughout the year.
Legal authorization to work in the United States.

Nice-to-have Requirements

Experience founding or co-founding a business.
Background in coaching, mentoring, or directing programs for teenagers or young adults.
An existing network of founders, investors, operators, or mentors.
Prior experience in early-stage startups where cross-functional responsibilities were routine.

About Founders School

Founders School is a four-year high school built for ambitious students who want to create, build, and solve meaningful problems. The program combines Alpha School's elite academics, real-world experience, and mentorship from world-class entrepreneurs around three commitments to every student: Build a million-dollar business. Identify problems in the world worth solving. Master AI before adults do.

This is a place for people who'd rather build the thing than manage the thing. We're hiring the founding team that builds the dream high school for the most entrepreneurial and ambitious students in the country. Launching in NYC Fall 2026.

jobs.description:
Come join our awesome team as the Executive Director with Valley Manor Care Center!

Valley Manor Care Center is part of Volunteers of America National Services which serves as the Housing and Healthcare subsidiary of the Volunteers of America parent organization.

Hourly Pay:
$118k-$130k annually with previous work experience!

Schedules:

The role of the Executive Director
requires a combination of organizational leadership and operational talent as well as strategic expertise. The Executive Director provides the management expertise for achieving the goals and objectives of the program in accordance with the mission of the organization. The Executive Director reports to the Regional Operations Director. Reporting to the Executive Director are various department supervisors and staff.

QUALIFICATIONS:

Bachelor’s degree in Healthcare Administration or related field; licensed Nursing Home Administrator (NHA) for the state of Colorado; State requirements may dictate supervision and degree type.
Minimum of five (5) years Management experience, preferably in a long-term care healthcare setting; knowledge of long-term healthcare and public health settings; familiarity with Federal and State regulations and requirements for long-term care.
Negotiation skills and ability to carry on several levels of reasoning at once during problem-solving sessions.
High degree of professionalism; ability to deal with extremely confidential information.
Proficient in Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, e-mail and the Internet.
Excellent communication skills.
Flexibility to work occasional weekends and holidays.
Ability to utilize computers and other electronic devices for tasks such as timekeeping, in-servicing and documentation.

In addition to managing the internal operations of the program, the Executive Director
must also be aware of conditions, events and issues that affect the community in which the program operates. Effective fulfillment of this broad responsibility requires awareness, involvement and insight; functioning as an agent for change; and a willingness to take risks and accept accountability for their outcomes within the context of a sound organizational structure and management processes. The Executive Director should serve as the catalyst, facilitator, coordinator and communicator to ensure that strategies to meet goals and objectives are developed, programs are carried out, quality is monitored and continually improved, and the results are evaluated. The Executive Director is expected to infuse the mission and philosophy of the organization into the program.

ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS:

The Executive Director’s
responsibilities can be divided into six (6) broad categories: planning; organization; operations/management; financial resources; human resources; and leadership.

Planning
The Executive Director engages in a planning process for the program that determines its goals and objectives in relationship to changes in the environment and the needs of its community, establishes priorities, frames the most appropriate organizational structure to achieve its goals and objectives, and provides benchmarks for evaluating the achievement of its goals and objectives. The results of the planning process should be reflected in a business plan, developed by the Executive Director.
The Executive Director should ensure that the business plan is based on an assessment that reflects an adequate database on the needs of the community, an evaluation of trends, legislative activity, and documentation of demographics, utilization and financial trends. This assessment should be linked with an ongoing assessment of the strengths and weaknesses of the program’s services in relation to those of other providers and to the needs of the community.
The business plan is supported by an effective public relations program, developed by the Executive Director that builds community understanding and support of the program and services and those of the national organization.

Organization
The Executive Director should create and monitor the organizational structure of the program to ensure its effectiveness and responsiveness to those being served. The nature of the structure is dynamic and flexible, responsive to needs of the participants. The Executive Director should develop an organizational plan that clearly assigns responsibilities for the program’s services to functional departments and to individuals. The Executive Director should develop, recommend and implement a plan for continuity that ensures ongoing stability of the program. Such a plan should include performance standards that are stated in terms of continuous improvement targets, opportunities for internal and external development, and a system for evaluating the performance levels of all employees, consultants and other service providers.

Operations (Management)
The amount of time that can be allocated to essential activities that call upon the visionary and strategic skills of the Executive Director is dependent upon the effectiveness of the day-to-day operations of the program. The Executive Director must create, sustain and evaluate on an ongoing basis an infrastructure of people who take overall responsibility for implementing the program’s service strategy and for initiating corrections that keep the strategy on course.
The infrastructure should be supported by the organization’s general policies and guidelines, and by program-specific systems and processes that are customer-responsive, efficient and continually improved by collecting and analyzing relevant data. These systems and processes must include mechanisms that ensure compliance with the regulations, standards and criteria of various health, governmental, professional and accrediting agencies.
Ensure the facility completes a facility wide assessment to determine what resources are necessary to care for its residents competently during both day to day operations and emergencies. Facility must review and update the assessment, as necessary and at least annually.
Ensures the facility has developed, implemented, and maintains an effective comprehensive, data drive QAPI program that focuses on indicators of the outcomes of care and quality of life.
The Executive Director serves as permanent chair of the program’s Quality Council, the body charged with overseeing the implementation of the Quality Plan, allocation of resources, initiation of project team solutions and monitoring results.
Ensures the facilities has developed an Antibiotic Stewardship Program that promotes the appropriate use of antibiotics.
The Executive Director serves as the Facility Compliance Officer, responsible for implementation of the corporate compliance program and all compliance procedures related to operations of the facility.

Financial Resource Management
The Executive Director is responsible for the cost effective management of the program’s resources, and for implementing the organizational financial policies. This responsibility requires a commitment to provide the most economical and highest quality services possible in keeping with available resources.
As a not-for-profit provider, the organization’s long-term financial stability and capacity to address the diverse needs of those it serves is dependent upon the financial support of contributors who embrace its mission. The Executive Director is responsible for developing and implementing a program-specific fundraising plan (as part of the business plan) that projects the values of the total organization into the community, and ensures the community’s financial support of the program.

Human Resources
The Executive Director should cultivate and promote a work atmosphere that recognizes the vital importance of human resources to the organization. The work atmosphere should empower employees with the tools and autonomy needed to make key service improvements that are in the best interests of the organization and its customers. The Executive Director is responsible for instituting a plan for recruitment, hiring, retention, and development of effective personnel to attain the objectives and goals of the program. The plan should include strategies that effectively build the level of employee morale and esprit de corps necessary to facilitate problem solving, productivity and service excellence. The Executive Director involves managers in developing specific action plans for improving service that include goal-setting and skill-building.

Leadership
The volatility of the health services environment requires that the Executive Director demonstrate a strong proficiency in the ability to manage and effect change. The Executive Director can best achieve and sustain an influential position of shaping the structure and dynamics of the program by developing sharp communication skills, thinking proactively rather than reactively, networking with diversified community and professional representatives, serving in advisory and board positions, and being active in health-related service organizations.
Serve as enforcer of the Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, and the Age Discrimination Act of 1975.
Attends required in-services and completes assigned online modules.

At Valley Manor Care Center
in Montrose, CO, our aim is to find friendly and compassionate staff who are specially trained to care for patients' needs as individuals, respect their privacy, and help clients maintain as much independence as possible. While providing care to our patients, we want our staff to feel cared for and love their work while maintaining a work-life balance, so offering employees paid-time off benefits is a part of our comprehensive benefits package Our diverse and involved staff members are supported by the commitment to maintain relationships of functional trust with every team member, to always practice solutions-focused problem solving, and to continually affirm each team member’s contribution to the quality of our work.

Volunteers of America National Services has been certified as a Great Place to Work for 7 years running by its employees! Their work has a special meaning: this is not “just a job”.

Take pride in helping others and join us today!

At VOANS
, we celebrate sharing, encouraging and embracing diversity. Equal employment opportunities are available to all without regard to race, color, religion, sex, pregnancy, national origin, age, physical and mental disability, marital status, parental status, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, genetic information, military and veteran status, and any other characteristic protected by applicable law. We believe that blending individual strengths and unique personal differences nurtures and supports our organizations’ shared commitment to our mission and creates an inclusive and diverse environment where everyone feels valued and has the opportunity to do their personal best.

jobs.title: Youth Program Director, Waypoint Academy - $150,000/year USD
jobs.description:
On-site role at Waypoint Academy, Dripping Springs, Texas
$150,000 annual salary as a W2 employee with weekly pay. Health benefits begin day one.
Relocation assistance provided

The greatest support you can offer a young person is to place a challenge in front of them and resist the urge to carry it for them. If that philosophy troubles you, this isn't the right fit. If it resonates, read on.

Waypoint Academy runs core academics through self-paced, AI-driven adaptive platforms that consolidate the school day into a concentrated morning block. There are no traditional lectures. This structure liberates your time for the work that genuinely shifts a student's path. You might start your morning in a one-on-one strategy session with a Guide, analyzing student metrics to identify where a group is faltering. By midday, you're outside facilitating a hands-on workshop that builds teamwork and grit through authentic struggle. Afternoons are dedicated to motivational coaching: meeting individually with students, uncovering what inspires them, and mobilizing a reluctant learner toward meaningful goals. You maintain your own student cohort throughout, ensuring the competencies you develop in others remain grounded in current practice.

You've likely been the outlier in traditional education: too metric-oriented, too outcome-focused, too comfortable taking students outdoors and allowing them to grapple with difficulty. This could be the first environment that truly aligns. You'll mentor other Guides from the outset, and as your results compound, the benchmark you establish influences the entire cohort and your authority within the program expands. The impact is tangible and immediate: a doubtful student who begins to claim their own development, a Guide who sharpens because you offered candid feedback, a young person who emerges from the woods more resilient than when they entered.

Uphold the standard. Lead them outdoors. Apply today.

What You Will Be Doing

Mentor the Guides working with you: analyze performance metrics, observe their interactions with students, and conclude each session with targeted actions that address identified gaps.
Facilitate one-hour outdoor life-skills workshops where students in grades K-12 develop leadership, grit, collaboration, and communication through adventure-based activities.
Conduct motivational coaching sessions informed by student analytics and Coachbot insights to activate every learner toward their objectives and cultivate ownership of their progress.
Maintain your own student cohort while guiding other educators, ensuring your coaching is rooted in present experience rather than past recollection.
Monitor student satisfaction, goal achievement, and Guide effectiveness on a weekly basis to identify and address underperformance early.

What You Won’t Be Doing

Delivering lectures or providing academic tutoring. Students engage with content via self-directed adaptive platforms, frequently without direct adult supervision.
Creating curriculum or lesson plans from the ground up. Established playbooks are in place; success depends on faithful execution and engagement, not innovation.
Training Guides on instructional methods. Since Guides don't lecture, your coaching focuses on motivation, accountability, and relational presence with students.
Compromising standards to preserve comfort. You won't advance a student prematurely or overlook a Guide's lack of performance.
Confining students indoors to desks. A substantial portion of development here occurs outside, through experiential challenge.

Youth Program Director Key Responsibilities

Ensure that every student in your cohort loves school, achieves academic growth, and develops the character and life skills necessary to succeed.

Basic Requirements

Willing to work on-site at Waypoint Academy in Dripping Springs, Texas
Bachelor's degree in education, psychology, business, outdoor education, recreation, youth development, or a related discipline
Minimum of 5 years of experience in education, youth development, coaching, outdoor education, or learning and development (L&D)
Demonstrated history of leading a team of 5 or more adults, including making hiring and performance-based termination decisions
Hands-on experience working with youth ages 5-18 in an educational or developmental capacity
Experience building, launching, or substantially growing a program, team, school, or outdoor initiative from inception, with a concrete and measurable example
Documented use of data and metrics to establish goals and inform decisions, not simply to report outcomes
Legal authorization to work in the United States

Nice-to-have Requirements

Master's degree in a relevant field
Experience coaching athletics, leading wilderness or experiential programs, directing camps, or managing youth programs at a scale requiring cohort management and outcome measurement
Transitioned from classroom teaching to build or manage programs in a different capacity: EdTech, tutoring, youth nonprofits, or school intervention teams
Early-career history of exceptional personal performance—academic, athletic, or professional—prior to entering leadership
Conviction that the conventional education model is fundamentally flawed and personal commitment to its transformation

About Waypoint Academy

Nature-based school that blends rigorous academics with real-world challenges.

jobs.title: Director of Development
jobs.description:
Director of Development

Full-Time · On-Site · Philadelphia, PA · $175K–$225K OTE · Partner Track
Design Pro Development is scaling its entitlement pipeline to as many as 30 active projects per year across Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and Camden County, NJ — including our largest project to date, a 300+ unit residential community, and roughly 10 semi-custom luxury homes ($2MM–$8MM) per year under our Atelier Design + Build division. We're hiring a Director of Development to own that pipeline end to end: every approval, every permit, every township agreement, and every dollar of sitework, on every project.
About Design Pro Development

DPD is a Philadelphia-based real estate development firm with 600+ residential units across ground-up construction, adaptive reuse, and condominium conversion in Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and southern New Jersey. We specialize in complex sites — off-market acquisitions, historic districts, zoning variances, and challenging entitlements — and move fast without cutting corners.
About the Role

Reporting to the CEO and VP of Capital Markets, you'll be a key decision maker leading projects from inception to stabilization. You'll build a standardized entitlement process — acquisition due diligence through zoning, land development approval, and building permit — and run it across up to 30 simultaneous projects spanning multifamily, condominium conversion, and semi-custom luxury design-build, working independently with no construction administrator for the first 12–24 months. You interrogate every civil, geotechnical, and traffic recommendation, maximize each site's potential while driving down cost, and bring a regional network of zoning attorneys, township solicitors, architects, and engineers. You verify; you don't rubber-stamp.
What You'll Own

Due Diligence Oversight:
Key decision maker over due diligence — site conditions, entitlements, zoning, environmental factors, and financial feasibility before acquisition — coordinating with legal, design, and finance and reporting to the CEO.
Full Entitlement Lifecycle:
Carry up to 30 projects/year from due diligence through zoning relief, land development approval, and building permit across four counties — through construction, marketing/leasing, and stabilization — managing consultants and contractors and hitting KPIs.
Philadelphia Approvals:
L&I permitting, ZBA variances, Civic Design Review, RCO meetings, Historical Commission review, PWD stormwater, and Streets Department coordination.
Suburban Approvals:
Township land development (SALDO), conditional use and special exception hearings, zoning variances, planning commission and board of supervisors presentations, PennDOT HOPs, DEP/NPDES permits, and Act 537 sewer modules in Montgomery and Bucks Counties.
New Jersey Approvals:
Planning board and zoning board of adjustment applications under the NJ Municipal Land Use Law in Camden County — site plan and variance approvals, NJDEP permitting, and soil conservation district review.
Condominium Documents & Association Approvals:
Oversee condominium declarations, plats and plans, and public offering statements with counsel; present renovation plans to associations for board approval and negotiate document amendments.
Development & Utility Agreements:
Negotiate improvement agreements, township escrow and financial security terms, fee-in-lieu arrangements, and traffic/infrastructure contributions; secure service, extension, and tap agreements with water, sewer, and power providers (Aqua PA, PA American Water, PECO, PGW).
Engineering Diligence & Value Engineering:
Rigorously review engineer recommendations on grading, stormwater, utilities, and site layout, and use creative value engineering to optimize budgets and maximize financial success.
Sitework Management & Cost Control:
Manage site design/layout to cut sitework cost across clearing, cul-de-sac construction, utility and infrastructure installation, retaining walls, bridges, and stormwater facilities. Build independent budgets; solicit, level, and negotiate GC/sub bids; challenge unit pricing and quantities; and guard against overpricing, scope gaps, and change-order creep.
AIA Contracts & Document Integrity:
Draft, review, negotiate, and administer AIA agreements (A101/A102/A201, B101) — payment applications, lien-waiver tracking, change orders, and schedule enforcement — verifying the correct drawings, specs, and exhibits are attached so no exclusion shifts cost or risk onto the firm.
Design, Renderings & Sales Support:
Manage interior designers and finish/material selections to hit budgets and out-sales; direct rendering companies for entitlement and marketing visuals; and partner with luxury realtors and sales brokers.
Atelier Design + Build Delivery:
Shepherd ~10 semi-custom luxury design-build projects/year through permitting and contract administration. Build strong relationships with discerning $2MM–$8MM custom-home clients and deliver white-glove execution with financial discipline and on-time delivery.
Budget & Schedule Oversight:
Develop and maintain project budgets and schedules from due diligence through completion — forecasting, cost-saving strategies, and negotiating competitive vendor rates.
AI-Powered Pipeline Tracking:
Build and maintain AI-driven tracking systems (Claude, Asana automations, and related tools) that monitor every approval milestone and flag slipping timelines.
Stakeholder & Cross-Departmental Collaboration:
Build relationships with regulatory agencies, township engineers, and zoning and planning boards; collaborate with the CEO, finance, and VP of Capital Markets; and support strategic research and presentations.

What We're Looking For — Required

Bachelor's degree (Master's preferred) and 10+ years in real estate development or construction management in southeastern PA and/or southern NJ, with a track record across all phases of development.
100+ projects permitted (or 500,000+ sq ft), including 25+ zoning projects and 100+ homesites completed — and the ability to walk us through them.
Minimum 3 years in Montgomery or Bucks County developing homes selling for more than $2MM.
Direct experience entitling large multifamily (200+ units) through PA township land development, and hands-on sitework management on 5+ jobs.
An established regional network of zoning attorneys, township solicitors and engineers, architects, and civil engineers across Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and Camden Counties.
Working fluency in the PA Municipalities Planning Code, township SALDO and zoning ordinances, the Philadelphia Zoning Code, L&I permitting, and the NJ Municipal Land Use Law — plus a baseline command of construction and zoning law (mechanics' liens, risk allocation, vested rights, appeals).
Condominium creation and governance — PA Uniform Condominium Act and NJ Condominium Act, document drafting and amendment, and negotiating with association boards.
Hands-on experience negotiating development agreements, township escrow and financial security terms, and utility agreements with water, sewer, and power providers.
Owner-side AIA contract experience — drafting, reviewing, and negotiating documents, with expert command of contractor proposals, bid inclusions/exclusions, and complete exhibit packages.
Expert ability reading surveys, civil drawings, construction reports, contracts, and design documents, with the technical depth to independently estimate, qualify, and negotiate sitework costs.
Exceptional project budgeting and value-engineering skill; a creative yet risk-averse problem solver who enacts SOPs and safeguards, with acute attention to detail and a strong sense of urgency.
Strong leadership and communication skills for building relationships with contractors, design professionals, township engineers, and boards.
A masterful command of AI tools, Claude in particular, with real examples of AI for site analysis, cost forecasting, bid evaluation, rendering, estimation, and tracking. Show us what you've built.
Full-time, in-office role; must be able to drive 6–10 hours per week to jobsites.

Preferred

Civil engineering, land planning, construction management, or legal background (PE, JD, or AICP a plus).

new_jobs.description:
Come join our awesome team as the Executive Director with Valley Manor Care Center!

Valley Manor Care Center is part of Volunteers of America National Services which serves as the Housing and Healthcare subsidiary of the Volunteers of America parent organization.

Hourly Pay:
$118k-$130k annually with previous work experience!

Schedules:

Employer/Employee Benefits:

Medical, Dental and Vision insurance
Health Savings Account (HSA)
Flexible Saving Account (FSA)
403(b) - with discretionary contribution
Paid Vacation/Sick Time
Employee Referral Program

Benefits with minimal to no cost to employees:

Scholarships
Employee Assistance Program (EAP)
Wellness program
Life insurance (with an option to purchase additional)
Short term disability
Loan program
NetSpend – Get paid early: Tap into 50% of your earnings before payday.
Ministry Program

The role of the Executive Director
requires a combination of organizational leadership and operational talent as well as strategic expertise. The Executive Director provides the management expertise for achieving the goals and objectives of the program in accordance with the mission of the organization. The Executive Director reports to the Regional Operations Director. Reporting to the Executive Director are various department supervisors and staff.

QUALIFICATIONS:

Bachelor’s degree in Healthcare Administration or related field; licensed Nursing Home Administrator (NHA) for the state of Colorado; State requirements may dictate supervision and degree type.
Minimum of five (5) years Management experience, preferably in a long-term care healthcare setting; knowledge of long-term healthcare and public health settings; familiarity with Federal and State regulations and requirements for long-term care.
Negotiation skills and ability to carry on several levels of reasoning at once during problem-solving sessions.
High degree of professionalism; ability to deal with extremely confidential information.
Proficient in Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, e-mail and the Internet.
Excellent communication skills.
Flexibility to work occasional weekends and holidays.
Ability to utilize computers and other electronic devices for tasks such as timekeeping, in-servicing and documentation.

In addition to managing the internal operations of the program, the Executive Director
must also be aware of conditions, events and issues that affect the community in which the program operates. Effective fulfillment of this broad responsibility requires awareness, involvement and insight; functioning as an agent for change; and a willingness to take risks and accept accountability for their outcomes within the context of a sound organizational structure and management processes. The Executive Director should serve as the catalyst, facilitator, coordinator and communicator to ensure that strategies to meet goals and objectives are developed, programs are carried out, quality is monitored and continually improved, and the results are evaluated. The Executive Director is expected to infuse the mission and philosophy of the organization into the program.

ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS:

The Executive Director’s
responsibilities can be divided into six (6) broad categories: planning; organization; operations/management; financial resources; human resources; and leadership.

Planning
The Executive Director engages in a planning process for the program that determines its goals and objectives in relationship to changes in the environment and the needs of its community, establishes priorities, frames the most appropriate organizational structure to achieve its goals and objectives, and provides benchmarks for evaluating the achievement of its goals and objectives. The results of the planning process should be reflected in a business plan, developed by the Executive Director.
The Executive Director should ensure that the business plan is based on an assessment that reflects an adequate database on the needs of the community, an evaluation of trends, legislative activity, and documentation of demographics, utilization and financial trends. This assessment should be linked with an ongoing assessment of the strengths and weaknesses of the program’s services in relation to those of other providers and to the needs of the community.
The business plan is supported by an effective public relations program, developed by the Executive Director that builds community understanding and support of the program and services and those of the national organization.

Organization
The Executive Director should create and monitor the organizational structure of the program to ensure its effectiveness and responsiveness to those being served. The nature of the structure is dynamic and flexible, responsive to needs of the participants. The Executive Director should develop an organizational plan that clearly assigns responsibilities for the program’s services to functional departments and to individuals. The Executive Director should develop, recommend and implement a plan for continuity that ensures ongoing stability of the program. Such a plan should include performance standards that are stated in terms of continuous improvement targets, opportunities for internal and external development, and a system for evaluating the performance levels of all employees, consultants and other service providers.

Operations (Management)
The amount of time that can be allocated to essential activities that call upon the visionary and strategic skills of the Executive Director is dependent upon the effectiveness of the day-to-day operations of the program. The Executive Director must create, sustain and evaluate on an ongoing basis an infrastructure of people who take overall responsibility for implementing the program’s service strategy and for initiating corrections that keep the strategy on course.
The infrastructure should be supported by the organization’s general policies and guidelines, and by program-specific systems and processes that are customer-responsive, efficient and continually improved by collecting and analyzing relevant data. These systems and processes must include mechanisms that ensure compliance with the regulations, standards and criteria of various health, governmental, professional and accrediting agencies.
Ensure the facility completes a facility wide assessment to determine what resources are necessary to care for its residents competently during both day to day operations and emergencies. Facility must review and update the assessment, as necessary and at least annually.
Ensures the facility has developed, implemented, and maintains an effective comprehensive, data drive QAPI program that focuses on indicators of the outcomes of care and quality of life.
The Executive Director serves as permanent chair of the program’s Quality Council, the body charged with overseeing the implementation of the Quality Plan, allocation of resources, initiation of project team solutions and monitoring results.
Ensures the facilities has developed an Antibiotic Stewardship Program that promotes the appropriate use of antibiotics.
The Executive Director serves as the Facility Compliance Officer, responsible for implementation of the corporate compliance program and all compliance procedures related to operations of the facility.

Financial Resource Management
The Executive Director is responsible for the cost effective management of the program’s resources, and for implementing the organizational financial policies. This responsibility requires a commitment to provide the most economical and highest quality services possible in keeping with available resources.
As a not-for-profit provider, the organization’s long-term financial stability and capacity to address the diverse needs of those it serves is dependent upon the financial support of contributors who embrace its mission. The Executive Director is responsible for developing and implementing a program-specific fundraising plan (as part of the business plan) that projects the values of the total organization into the community, and ensures the community’s financial support of the program.

Human Resources
The Executive Director should cultivate and promote a work atmosphere that recognizes the vital importance of human resources to the organization. The work atmosphere should empower employees with the tools and autonomy needed to make key service improvements that are in the best interests of the organization and its customers. The Executive Director is responsible for instituting a plan for recruitment, hiring, retention, and development of effective personnel to attain the objectives and goals of the program. The plan should include strategies that effectively build the level of employee morale and esprit de corps necessary to facilitate problem solving, productivity and service excellence. The Executive Director involves managers in developing specific action plans for improving service that include goal-setting and skill-building.

Leadership
The volatility of the health services environment requires that the Executive Director demonstrate a strong proficiency in the ability to manage and effect change. The Executive Director can best achieve and sustain an influential position of shaping the structure and dynamics of the program by developing sharp communication skills, thinking proactively rather than reactively, networking with diversified community and professional representatives, serving in advisory and board positions, and being active in health-related service organizations.
Serve as enforcer of the Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, and the Age Discrimination Act of 1975.
Attends required in-services and completes assigned online modules.

At Valley Manor Care Center
in Montrose, CO, our aim is to find friendly and compassionate staff who are specially trained to care for patients' needs as individuals, respect their privacy, and help clients maintain as much independence as possible. While providing care to our patients, we want our staff to feel cared for and love their work while maintaining a work-life balance, so offering employees paid-time off benefits is a part of our comprehensive benefits package Our diverse and involved staff members are supported by the commitment to maintain relationships of functional trust with every team member, to always practice solutions-focused problem solving, and to continually affirm each team member’s contribution to the quality of our work.

Volunteers of America National Services has been certified as a Great Place to Work for 7 years running by its employees! Their work has a special meaning: this is not “just a job”.

Take pride in helping others and join us today!

At VOANS
, we celebrate sharing, encouraging and embracing diversity. Equal employment opportunities are available to all without regard to race, color, religion, sex, pregnancy, national origin, age, physical and mental disability, marital status, parental status, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, genetic information, military and veteran status, and any other characteristic protected by applicable law. We believe that blending individual strengths and unique personal differences nurtures and supports our organizations’ shared commitment to our mission and creates an inclusive and diverse environment where everyone feels valued and has the opportunity to do their personal best.

new_jobs.title: Youth Program Director, Waypoint Academy - $150,000/year USD
new_jobs.description:
On-site role at Waypoint Academy, Dripping Springs, Texas
$150,000 annual salary as a W2 employee with weekly pay. Health benefits begin day one.
Relocation assistance provided

The greatest support you can offer a young person is to place a challenge in front of them and resist the urge to carry it for them. If that philosophy troubles you, this isn't the right fit. If it resonates, read on.

Waypoint Academy runs core academics through self-paced, AI-driven adaptive platforms that consolidate the school day into a concentrated morning block. There are no traditional lectures. This structure liberates your time for the work that genuinely shifts a student's path. You might start your morning in a one-on-one strategy session with a Guide, analyzing student metrics to identify where a group is faltering. By midday, you're outside facilitating a hands-on workshop that builds teamwork and grit through authentic struggle. Afternoons are dedicated to motivational coaching: meeting individually with students, uncovering what inspires them, and mobilizing a reluctant learner toward meaningful goals. You maintain your own student cohort throughout, ensuring the competencies you develop in others remain grounded in current practice.

You've likely been the outlier in traditional education: too metric-oriented, too outcome-focused, too comfortable taking students outdoors and allowing them to grapple with difficulty. This could be the first environment that truly aligns. You'll mentor other Guides from the outset, and as your results compound, the benchmark you establish influences the entire cohort and your authority within the program expands. The impact is tangible and immediate: a doubtful student who begins to claim their own development, a Guide who sharpens because you offered candid feedback, a young person who emerges from the woods more resilient than when they entered.

Uphold the standard. Lead them outdoors. Apply today.

What You Will Be Doing

Mentor the Guides working with you: analyze performance metrics, observe their interactions with students, and conclude each session with targeted actions that address identified gaps.
Facilitate one-hour outdoor life-skills workshops where students in grades K-12 develop leadership, grit, collaboration, and communication through adventure-based activities.
Conduct motivational coaching sessions informed by student analytics and Coachbot insights to activate every learner toward their objectives and cultivate ownership of their progress.
Maintain your own student cohort while guiding other educators, ensuring your coaching is rooted in present experience rather than past recollection.
Monitor student satisfaction, goal achievement, and Guide effectiveness on a weekly basis to identify and address underperformance early.

What You Won’t Be Doing

Delivering lectures or providing academic tutoring. Students engage with content via self-directed adaptive platforms, frequently without direct adult supervision.
Creating curriculum or lesson plans from the ground up. Established playbooks are in place; success depends on faithful execution and engagement, not innovation.
Training Guides on instructional methods. Since Guides don't lecture, your coaching focuses on motivation, accountability, and relational presence with students.
Compromising standards to preserve comfort. You won't advance a student prematurely or overlook a Guide's lack of performance.
Confining students indoors to desks. A substantial portion of development here occurs outside, through experiential challenge.

Youth Program Director Key Responsibilities

Ensure that every student in your cohort loves school, achieves academic growth, and develops the character and life skills necessary to succeed.

Basic Requirements

Willing to work on-site at Waypoint Academy in Dripping Springs, Texas
Bachelor's degree in education, psychology, business, outdoor education, recreation, youth development, or a related discipline
Minimum of 5 years of experience in education, youth development, coaching, outdoor education, or learning and development (L&D)
Demonstrated history of leading a team of 5 or more adults, including making hiring and performance-based termination decisions
Hands-on experience working with youth ages 5-18 in an educational or developmental capacity
Experience building, launching, or substantially growing a program, team, school, or outdoor initiative from inception, with a concrete and measurable example
Documented use of data and metrics to establish goals and inform decisions, not simply to report outcomes
Legal authorization to work in the United States

Nice-to-have Requirements

Master's degree in a relevant field
Experience coaching athletics, leading wilderness or experiential programs, directing camps, or managing youth programs at a scale requiring cohort management and outcome measurement
Transitioned from classroom teaching to build or manage programs in a different capacity: EdTech, tutoring, youth nonprofits, or school intervention teams
Early-career history of exceptional personal performance—academic, athletic, or professional—prior to entering leadership
Conviction that the conventional education model is fundamentally flawed and personal commitment to its transformation

About Waypoint Academy

Nature-based school that blends rigorous academics with real-world challenges.

There is so much to cover for this exciting role, and space here is limited. Hit the Apply button if you found this interesting and want to learn more. We look forward to meeting you!

Working with us

new_jobs.title: Director of Development
new_jobs.description:
Director of Development

Full-Time · On-Site · Philadelphia, PA · $175K–$225K OTE · Partner Track
Design Pro Development is scaling its entitlement pipeline to as many as 30 active projects per year across Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and Camden County, NJ — including our largest project to date, a 300+ unit residential community, and roughly 10 semi-custom luxury homes ($2MM–$8MM) per year under our Atelier Design + Build division. We're hiring a Director of Development to own that pipeline end to end: every approval, every permit, every township agreement, and every dollar of sitework, on every project.
About Design Pro Development

DPD is a Philadelphia-based real estate development firm with 600+ residential units across ground-up construction, adaptive reuse, and condominium conversion in Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and southern New Jersey. We specialize in complex sites — off-market acquisitions, historic districts, zoning variances, and challenging entitlements — and move fast without cutting corners.
About the Role

Reporting to the CEO and VP of Capital Markets, you'll be a key decision maker leading projects from inception to stabilization. You'll build a standardized entitlement process — acquisition due diligence through zoning, land development approval, and building permit — and run it across up to 30 simultaneous projects spanning multifamily, condominium conversion, and semi-custom luxury design-build, working independently with no construction administrator for the first 12–24 months. You interrogate every civil, geotechnical, and traffic recommendation, maximize each site's potential while driving down cost, and bring a regional network of zoning attorneys, township solicitors, architects, and engineers. You verify; you don't rubber-stamp.
What You'll Own

Due Diligence Oversight:
Key decision maker over due diligence — site conditions, entitlements, zoning, environmental factors, and financial feasibility before acquisition — coordinating with legal, design, and finance and reporting to the CEO.
Full Entitlement Lifecycle:
Carry up to 30 projects/year from due diligence through zoning relief, land development approval, and building permit across four counties — through construction, marketing/leasing, and stabilization — managing consultants and contractors and hitting KPIs.
Philadelphia Approvals:
L&I permitting, ZBA variances, Civic Design Review, RCO meetings, Historical Commission review, PWD stormwater, and Streets Department coordination.
Suburban Approvals:
Township land development (SALDO), conditional use and special exception hearings, zoning variances, planning commission and board of supervisors presentations, PennDOT HOPs, DEP/NPDES permits, and Act 537 sewer modules in Montgomery and Bucks Counties.
New Jersey Approvals:
Planning board and zoning board of adjustment applications under the NJ Municipal Land Use Law in Camden County — site plan and variance approvals, NJDEP permitting, and soil conservation district review.
Condominium Documents & Association Approvals:
Oversee condominium declarations, plats and plans, and public offering statements with counsel; present renovation plans to associations for board approval and negotiate document amendments.
Development & Utility Agreements:
Negotiate improvement agreements, township escrow and financial security terms, fee-in-lieu arrangements, and traffic/infrastructure contributions; secure service, extension, and tap agreements with water, sewer, and power providers (Aqua PA, PA American Water, PECO, PGW).
Engineering Diligence & Value Engineering:
Rigorously review engineer recommendations on grading, stormwater, utilities, and site layout, and use creative value engineering to optimize budgets and maximize financial success.
Sitework Management & Cost Control:
Manage site design/layout to cut sitework cost across clearing, cul-de-sac construction, utility and infrastructure installation, retaining walls, bridges, and stormwater facilities. Build independent budgets; solicit, level, and negotiate GC/sub bids; challenge unit pricing and quantities; and guard against overpricing, scope gaps, and change-order creep.
AIA Contracts & Document Integrity:
Draft, review, negotiate, and administer AIA agreements (A101/A102/A201, B101) — payment applications, lien-waiver tracking, change orders, and schedule enforcement — verifying the correct drawings, specs, and exhibits are attached so no exclusion shifts cost or risk onto the firm.
Design, Renderings & Sales Support:
Manage interior designers and finish/material selections to hit budgets and out-sales; direct rendering companies for entitlement and marketing visuals; and partner with luxury realtors and sales brokers.
Atelier Design + Build Delivery:
Shepherd ~10 semi-custom luxury design-build projects/year through permitting and contract administration. Build strong relationships with discerning $2MM–$8MM custom-home clients and deliver white-glove execution with financial discipline and on-time delivery.
Budget & Schedule Oversight:
Develop and maintain project budgets and schedules from due diligence through completion — forecasting, cost-saving strategies, and negotiating competitive vendor rates.
AI-Powered Pipeline Tracking:
Build and maintain AI-driven tracking systems (Claude, Asana automations, and related tools) that monitor every approval milestone and flag slipping timelines.
Stakeholder & Cross-Departmental Collaboration:
Build relationships with regulatory agencies, township engineers, and zoning and planning boards; collaborate with the CEO, finance, and VP of Capital Markets; and support strategic research and presentations.

What We're Looking For — Required

Bachelor's degree (Master's preferred) and 10+ years in real estate development or construction management in southeastern PA and/or southern NJ, with a track record across all phases of development.
100+ projects permitted (or 500,000+ sq ft), including 25+ zoning projects and 100+ homesites completed — and the ability to walk us through them.
Minimum 3 years in Montgomery or Bucks County developing homes selling for more than $2MM.
Direct experience entitling large multifamily (200+ units) through PA township land development, and hands-on sitework management on 5+ jobs.
An established regional network of zoning attorneys, township solicitors and engineers, architects, and civil engineers across Philadelphia, Montgomery, Bucks, and Camden Counties.
Working fluency in the PA Municipalities Planning Code, township SALDO and zoning ordinances, the Philadelphia Zoning Code, L&I permitting, and the NJ Municipal Land Use Law — plus a baseline command of construction and zoning law (mechanics' liens, risk allocation, vested rights, appeals).
Condominium creation and governance — PA Uniform Condominium Act and NJ Condominium Act, document drafting and amendment, and negotiating with association boards.
Hands-on experience negotiating development agreements, township escrow and financial security terms, and utility agreements with water, sewer, and power providers.
Owner-side AIA contract experience — drafting, reviewing, and negotiating documents, with expert command of contractor proposals, bid inclusions/exclusions, and complete exhibit packages.
Expert ability reading surveys, civil drawings, construction reports, contracts, and design documents, with the technical depth to independently estimate, qualify, and negotiate sitework costs.
Exceptional project budgeting and value-engineering skill; a creative yet risk-averse problem solver who enacts SOPs and safeguards, with acute attention to detail and a strong sense of urgency.
Strong leadership and communication skills for building relationships with contractors, design professionals, township engineers, and boards.
A masterful command of AI tools, Claude in particular, with real examples of AI for site analysis, cost forecasting, bid evaluation, rendering, estimation, and tracking. Show us what you've built.
Full-time, in-office role; must be able to drive 6–10 hours per week to jobsites.

Preferred

Civil engineering, land planning, construction management, or legal background (PE, JD, or AICP a plus).
Experience standing up entitlement processes and reporting systems at a scaling development firm.

Software Proficiency

Proficient in most of the following (quick to pick up the rest): Claude (AI tracking, follow-up, document review); Asana (multi-project pipeline tracking and automations — our standard); Zapier or Make; Bluebeam Revu (plan review, markups, takeoffs); AutoCAD / Civil 3D; Procore, BuilderTrend, or Autodesk Build; Microsoft Project or Primavera P6; advanced Excel (sitework budgets, bid leveling, proforma); eCLIPSE and Atlas (Philadelphia L&I/records); PennDOT ePermitting (EPS); PA DEP GreenPort and NJDEP; ArcGIS or municipal GIS parcel viewers; PlanSwift/STACK; DocuSign; Adobe Acrobat Pro; and Microsoft Office.
What We Offer

Total compensation of $175,000–$225,000 — a $125,000 base plus quarterly performance and project-based profit-sharing of $50,000–$100,000.
Competitive benefits: medical, short/long-term disability, life insurance, and a 401(k) plan.
Ability to invest in company projects.
A structured pathway to become a Partner after five years.

How to Apply

Email your resume and a Word document with links to photos or project websites to logan@designprodev.com, erica@designprodev.com, and alex@designprodev.com. Include the 10 projects (1–2 sentences each, based on your role) that best exemplify your abilities — the approvals you secured, the timelines you hit, and the sitework dollars you saved — plus at least one AI-powered tracking system or workflow you built and what it caught. Qualified candidates are contacted within three business days.

new_jobs.title: Director of Advancement, Alpha - $400,000/year USD
new_jobs.description:
Families at Alpha Miami rely on trusted advisors across every dimension of their lives. You will become the one they have been missing.

You will be the person they reach out to when questions arise, when intuition signals a concern, or when they want to share a milestone with someone who truly understands. You will develop a deep familiarity with each family—enough to sense their needs before they articulate them—and you will bring the judgment, poise, and grace to respond with discretion and ease. Over time, you will become more than a representative of their child's school. You will be woven into the way they experience this formative period of their family's journey.

Miami's private school landscape is intimate, tightly networked, and unforgiving of errors. A family's Alpha experience does not remain behind closed doors—it circulates through the channels that define influence: residential buildings, private clubs, social gatherings, the gala calendar, and the WhatsApp threads that determine where the next family enrolls. You will navigate this terrain intuitively, drawing on a career spent in environments where reputation is the sole measure of value and a single lapse reverberates indefinitely. You will cultivate a community that families defend and champion, where inclusion feels natural and referrals emerge organically from the strength of the experience.

Miami operates as a global hub by nature. You will engage with families from Latin America, international corporate leaders, finance and technology professionals newly anchored in Miami, and globally mobile households whose educational expectations—and institutional relationships—are informed by contexts well beyond the American independent school model. Many transition fluidly between Miami, São Paulo, Bogotá, Madrid, and New York as readily as others commute locally, and they will evaluate Alpha against alternatives worldwide. The ideal candidate will navigate cross-cultural family dynamics and the expectation of seamless, sophisticated engagement as confidently as they do the established patterns of Miami's private school community.

Alpha represents a departure from conventional schooling. Students complete core academic work in two hours daily through AI-powered applications, then dedicate remaining time to public speaking, critical thinking, and applied projects. No traditional lectures. No unnecessary assignments. Top 1% outcomes nationally.

The families who select Alpha have made a deliberate choice—and in a city where opinions are abundant and educational decisions signal status, they will be asked to justify it repeatedly. You will ensure they are never without a confident response. If sustaining that conviction through years of discerning, globally informed scrutiny feels daunting, this position is not the right match. If it represents the most compelling professional challenge you can envision, continue reading.

What You Will Be Doing

Shape the cultural fabric of the school community—the atmosphere, rituals, and implicit norms that foster a sense of belonging—so Alpha becomes a place families take pride in, not merely a school they selected
Steward the entire parent relationship arc—from initial contact through enrollment, ongoing retention, and sustained advocacy—so faculty remain concentrated on students and families always have a reliable point of contact
Detect early signals when dynamics shift, and address them with the care and judgment that preserve confidence—including the more challenging determination of when a family is not aligned with Alpha's model
Maintain visibility where families gather: morning arrivals, afternoon departures, evening programs, and the spontaneous exchanges that carry the most weight
Develop a community that both domestic and international families actively champion—and where word-of-mouth referrals flow naturally from the quality of the experience
Communicate Alpha's narrative persuasively—in individual meetings, at community events, and in the sophisticated, globally connected circles where the school's standing is truly established

What You Won’t Be Doing

Operating from behind a desk—your physical presence within the community defines the role
Adhering to standard 9–5 schedules—early mornings, late afternoons, and evening programming are inherent to the position, not occasional demands
Escalating challenging parent discussions to faculty or administration—you assume full ownership of these interactions
Relying on administrative infrastructure, predefined workflows, or a complete team—you function with considerable autonomy and construct what is necessary
Approaching family relationships as transactional accounts—this role succeeds through authentic engagement, not procedural adherence

Director Of Advancement Key Responsibilities

Serve as the central trusted relationship for every family at Alpha's Miami campus, and the architect of the surrounding community culture. Sustain their confidence through challenging moments, establish the traditions and tone that make belonging meaningful, and create the environment in which satisfied families become articulate advocates within the networks that influence Miami's private education landscape.

Basic Requirements

5+ years building and maintaining trust with high-net-worth or ultra-high-net-worth families in a relationship-intensive, high-touch capacity
Demonstrated success guiding discerning clients through consequential decisions—with measurable outcomes you can reference: conversion rates, retention figures, referral volume
The discretion and steadiness to navigate sensitive, confidential matters without leaving visible impact
Proven experience managing and developing people—mentoring, upholding rigorous standards, and executing difficult personnel decisions—not simply managing relationships or client accounts
Innate cross-cultural fluency with globally mobile families and established Miami residents
AI-forward orientation—you use AI tools with ease, understand their strengths and limitations, and actively introduce improved solutions, even if you are not building them directly
Located in Miami, available for full-time on-site engagement, and adaptable to non-standard hours
Authorized to work in the U.S. without requiring sponsorship

Nice-to-have Requirements

Background as a head of school, principal, superintendent, or equivalent school leadership position
Professional proficiency in Spanish or Portuguese
Personal or professional exposure to independent or non-traditional educational models
Established connections within Miami's private school parent communities
Experience serving internationally mobile, Latin American, or globally connected family populations
A personal network extending into the neighborhoods, clubs, and institutions where Alpha's families reside and make decisions

About Alpha

Join the team building school around student growth

Alpha reimagines the school day around faster learning, stronger mentorship, and more time for students to build real-world skills.

Roles with real student impact:
Alpha hires teachers (guides), operators, admissions experts, school & curriculum leaders, and remote team members.

Education work that pays like it matters:
Alpha offers six-figure roles for people ready to help build a radically better school experience.

A culture built for builders:
Alpha is fast-moving, ambitious, and student focused. It’s for people who want to improve the system, not maintain it.

Alpha is where you can do the most meaningful work of your career.

The most impactful thing you can do for a student who scored 99% is tell them it isn't passing. If that idea makes you uncomfortable, this role isn't for you. If it resonates, read on.

At Alpha, students complete their academic work in two hours daily using self-directed, AI-driven applications. There are no teachers. No lectures. No classroom-based instruction. This structure allows you to focus on what truly shifts outcomes: facilitating high-intensity workshops on communication, concentration, and critique; using live analytics to motivate students toward full goal attainment; and coaching the Guides who support your mission.

A typical morning might begin with a one-on-one Guide coaching conversation, analyzing performance data and pinpointing where a cohort is slipping. By midday you're facilitating a student workshop, executing a structured activity on constructive feedback exchange. Afternoons transition to direct motivation work: engaging individual students, uncovering their intrinsic drivers, and leveraging Alpha's incentive framework to re-engage a reluctant middle schooler. You manage your own cohort alongside this work, keeping your skills aligned with what you're teaching others.

You've likely been the outlier in traditional education: overly analytical, overly outcome-oriented, overly willing to question conventional practices around student development. Alpha may be the first environment that aligns with your approach. You'll begin coaching Guides immediately, and as you demonstrate scalable impact, your influence over program-wide standards will expand.

Uphold the standard. Transform the learner. Apply today.

What You Will Be Doing

Facilitating one-hour workshops on essential competencies (public speaking, concentration, feedback exchange) for elementary and middle school cohorts (K-8), delivering Alpha's established playbook with precision rather than personal interpretation
Conducting motivation interventions informed by student data and Alpha's incentive architecture (campus currency, performance leaderboards) to achieve 100% goal completion across your cohort

new_jobs.description:
On-site (multiple locations—see Requirements)
W2 Employee
$200K annually

The families who choose Alpha rely on trusted advisors across every aspect of their lives. This role represents the one they've been waiting for.

You will become the person they reach out to when uncertainty arises, when something doesn't feel quite right, or when they want to share a moment they know you'll appreciate. You'll develop enough familiarity with each family to anticipate their needs before they articulate them—and you'll possess the judgment and composure to address whatever they bring with discretion, warmth, and apparent ease. Over time, your role will extend beyond being part of their child's school. You'll become woven into the fabric of how they experience this chapter of their family journey.

This level of trust isn't built through process. It emerges from presence—daily, steady, unhurried. You'll maintain visibility during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up, at evening events that hold significance, and in the one-on-one exchanges that never appear on any schedule. You'll cultivate a community that families feel invested in protecting, where belonging feels natural and referrals occur because the experience merits them.

Alpha operates outside traditional school frameworks. Students complete their core academics in two hours daily using AI-powered applications, then dedicate remaining time to public speaking, critical thinking, and real-world projects. No lectures. No busywork. Top 1% outcomes nationally. The families selecting this model are investing in something they believe in—but belief requires ongoing nurture.

You'll sustain that dialogue with every family, through every moment of uncertainty, throughout their child's enrollment. If defending an unconventional approach to sophisticated skeptics feels burdensome, this position isn't suited to you. If it represents the most compelling conversation you could engage in, continue reading.

What You Will Be Doing

Take ownership of relationships with every family on campus—the continuous, evolving relationships that ensure parents feel recognized, not merely serviced
Identify concerns before they surface as conversations, and address them with the discretion and interpersonal skill that preserves trust
Maintain visible presence where families gather: morning drop-off (7–9am), afternoon pick-up (2–4pm), evening parent gatherings, and occasional weekend programming
Develop a campus community with authentic depth—events, traditions, and interactions that give families a sense of belonging to something worthy of protection
Nurture parent advocates naturally, creating experiences of such consistent quality that referrals emerge as an organic result
Sustain the ongoing dialogue about Alpha's AI-powered approach with families managing screen time questions, skeptical relatives, and occasional doubt—not only during enrollment, but continuously throughout their campus experience
Recognize when a family isn't well-suited and address that reality with the same thoughtfulness you apply elsewhere—safeguarding the community is integral to serving it
Establish the systems and cadences this position demands in a setting where the framework is still being developed

What You Won’t Be Doing

Handling enrollment—that falls under the Admissions Director's responsibility; your work commences when families enroll and deepens thereafter
Operating from behind a desk—your presence within the community defines the role
Maintaining standard 9–5 schedules—early mornings, late afternoons, and evening programming are inherent to the position, not occasional occurrences
Relying on administrative infrastructure, established protocols, or a complete team—you function with significant autonomy and create what's necessary
Approaching family relationships as accounts requiring management—this role rewards authentic connection, not procedural adherence

Director Of Strategic Partnerships Key Responsibilities

Serve as the trusted relationship anchor at the heart of every family's Alpha experience. Maintain their confidence through challenging moments, cultivate a community where belonging feels meaningful, and establish the conditions under which satisfied families become active advocates.

Basic Requirements

Currently live within commuting range of one of these campus locations OR prepared to relocate within 60 days (preference given to candidates with established local community ties): Denver, CO; Greenwich, CT; Boca Raton, FL; Tampa, FL; Boston, MA; Bethesda, MD; Charlotte, NC; Las Vegas, NV; Oklahoma City, OK; Tulsa, OK; Nashville, TN; Austin, TX; Dallas (Carrollton), TX; Fort Worth, TX; Houston (The Woodlands), TX; Park City, UT; Burlington, VT; Kirkland, WA; or Jackson Hole, WY
Authorized to work legally in the U.S. without sponsorship requirements
5+ years in a relationship-focused role serving high-net-worth or ultra-high-net-worth families—private school admissions, luxury hospitality, philanthropy, or any setting where discretion, warmth, and interpersonal sophistication were as critical as competence
Proven capacity to maintain relationships through challenging situations with sophisticated families and preserve trust throughout
Authentic presence and communication approach that builds confidence rapidly with affluent, high-expectation families—not polished in a manner that appears rehearsed
Personal connection to independent, private, or non-traditional education—through professional experience or as a parent—that provides genuine credibility when families challenge the model
Authentic conviction in AI-powered education and the capacity to sustain that belief credibly across years of parent relationships, not solely during enrollment discussions
Availability for non-standard hours: early mornings (7–9am), late afternoons (2–4pm), evening programming 2–3x monthly, and occasional weekend activities

Nice-to-have Requirements

Outstanding candidates will possess at least one of the following:

Established relationships within the local affluent community that would provide immediate, organic integration on campus
Private school or independent school background with demonstrated success in family retention and community building—not simply enrollment metrics
Experience managing high-stakes family relationships where your judgment and discretion were the foundation of sustained trust
Natural ability to design experiences—events, moments, settings—where individuals feel part of something worth safeguarding
Prior startup or early-stage environment experience where you built from the ground up and maintained composure through rapid change
